summary:
Germany's invasion of Poland on 1 September 1939 began [[event:112|World War II]] in Europe. The attack came after a series of staged provocations and a secret non-aggression pact between Nazi Germany and the Soviet Union (the Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact) that divided Eastern Europe into spheres of influence. German forces struck from the north, west, and south in a coordinated offensive featuring close air-ground cooperation — an early application of what would become known as Blitzkrieg (lightning war) tactics. Polish forces fought with great courage but were badly outmatched in numbers and technology.

Two days after the German invasion began, Britain and France honored their treaty commitments to Poland and declared war on Germany, transforming a regional conflict into a general European war. On 17 September, Soviet forces invaded eastern Poland from the east, as secretly agreed in the Molotov-Ribbentrop protocols. Poland was caught in a two-front vice; organized resistance largely collapsed by early October. The Polish government and military command fled into exile, eventually reconstituting in London, where they continued to fight alongside the Allies for the duration of the war.

Poland's fate under occupation was among the most brutal of any occupied nation. Under German rule in the west, the country became the primary site of the [[event:2660|Holocaust]], with extermination camps including Auschwitz-Birkenau, Treblinka, Sobibor, and Belzec situated on Polish soil. Millions of Polish Jews and non-Jews were murdered. Soviet-occupied eastern Poland saw mass deportations to Siberia and the Katyn massacre of thousands of Polish officers. By war's end, Poland had lost roughly six million citizens — about a fifth of its prewar population — a higher proportion than any other nation.
